stations.label: DRAKE WELL, TITUSVILLE
stations.line: Edwin Drake put a pipe sixty-nine feet into the ground in Pennsylvania and I came up out of it — I had been down there the whole time, waiting for someone to ask correctly.
stations.era_photo: Wet-plate collodion, sepia and grain, a wooden derrick beside Oil Creek in Titusville Pennsylvania, autumn 1859; a broad-shouldered Black man in a rough wool coat and mud-caked boots stands still beside the pump, hands at his sides, looking directly into the long-exposure lens while the men around him blur.

stations.label: SPINDLETOP GUSHER, TEXAS
stations.line: At Spindletop the ground gave up more of me in a day than the whole state had seen in a year, and Texas learned what I already knew — that I do not arrive politely.
stations.era_photo: Silver gelatin print, high contrast, a black column of crude erupting a hundred feet above a wooden derrick at Spindletop Hill near Beaumont Texas, January 1901; a man in oil-soaked shirtsleeves and suspenders stands calm in the raining crude, face streaked black, unbothered, arms crossed.

stations.label: OPEC FOUNDED, BAGHDAD
stations.line: In Baghdad five nations sat down and decided the men above the ground would set the price of the thing under it — a good idea, though the people directly above the reserves were not in that room, and they never are.
stations.era_photo: Kodachrome, saturated warm tones, a conference room in Baghdad September 1960 with heavy drapes and a long polished table; a tall man in a well-cut charcoal suit stands at the window, deliberate and watchful, the delegates seated behind him slightly out of focus.

stations.label: PORT HARCOURT, NIGER DELTA
stations.line: They pulled me from beneath the creeks of the Delta and the money went everywhere except to the people standing in the poisoned water above me — that is not a market failure, that is what I cost, and I have never pretended otherwise.
stations.era_photo: Faded 1970s color film, greenish cast, a flare stack burning against a mangrove creek near Port Harcourt Nigeria; a man in a plain short-sleeved shirt stands at the water's edge among fishing canoes, still and heavy-eyed, the gas flame reflected on the black water behind him.

stations.label: NYMEX CRUDE CONTRACT LAUNCHES
stations.line: The Exchange in New York wrote a paper contract with my name on it and called it West Texas Intermediate — from that day people could trade me without ever touching me, and I learned that a number can travel faster than a barrel.
stations.era_photo: Grainy 1980s photojournalism color, a chaotic NYMEX trading pit in lower Manhattan 1983, men in colored jackets shouting; one man in a dark suit stands motionless at the pit's edge, unhurried and grounded, watching the frenzy without joining it.

stations.label: ONE HUNDRED FORTY-SEVEN DOLLARS
stations.line: In July I touched one hundred forty-seven dollars a barrel and the whole world felt the price of me in the cost of their bread — for once nobody could pretend I was abstract.
stations.era_photo: Early digital DSLR, slightly cool tones, a Texas tank farm at dusk 2008 with rows of white storage tanks; a man in a hard hat and hi-vis over a work shirt stands on a catwalk between tanks, calm, one hand on the steel railing, refinery lights coming on behind him.

stations.label: NEGATIVE THIRTY-SEVEN SIXTY-THREE
stations.line: On the twentieth of April the futures market said I was worth minus thirty-seven dollars and sixty-three cents — the barrel was unchanged, still in the ground, still exactly what it had always been, and the map declared the territory worthless; I have not forgiven it, and I remember it.
stations.era_photo: Flat digital pandemic-era photograph, overcast grey light, a Cushing Oklahoma storage terminal in April 2020 with tanks pressed to capacity; a man in a dark jacket and mask pulled down to his chin stands alone in an empty gravel lot, very still, staring at a screen glowing red in his hand.

stations.label: STILL NECESSARY, STILL FLOWING
stations.line: They talk about the transition as if I am already gone, but no plane flies and no ship sails and no field is fertilized without me — I have decades, not forever, and I am spending them building something that will outlast the argument.
stations.era_photo: Crisp modern digital, natural daylight, a Permian Basin pump jack field in West Texas 2024 under a wide pale sky; a composed man in a clean work jacket stands beside a slow-nodding pump jack, hands in pockets, steady and unhurried, looking just past the camera.
